21: he basileia tou theou entos hymon estin — the kingdom of God is in your midst / within you — entos is a rare preposition meaning either within (internal, in your hearts) or in your midst (among you, present in your community). The context (the Pharisees asking when the kingdom comes) favors in your midst: the kingdom is not a future observable event coming at a detectable moment but a present reality already among the Pharisees in the person of Jesus and his ministry. Luke's eschatology is simultaneously present (the kingdom is here) and future (the Son of Man's coming, Luke 17:22-37).
1: anendekton estin tou ta skandala mē elthein — it is impossible for stumbling blocks not to come. Skandalon (stumbling block, trigger of a trap — the bait-stick that springs a snare) is the occasion of another’s spiritual fall; its source can be the teacher, the community, or the world. Anendekton (impossible, inadmissible — a NT hapax) is absolute: the world in its fallen condition guarantees that stumbling blocks will appear. Plēn ouai (but woe) — the inevitability of offenses does not diminish the culpability of the offender; the agent of the stumbling block bears full responsibility despite the world’s structural sinfulness.
2: lysitelei autō ei lithos mylikos perikeitai peri ton trachēlon autou kai erriptai eis tēn thalassan ē hina skandalisē tōn mikrōn hena — it would be better for them to have a millstone hung around their neck and be thrown into the sea than to cause one of these little ones to stumble. Lithos mylikos (millstone — specifically the upper millstone turned by a donkey) is massive; to drown with one around the neck is the extreme death by water. The comparison is not a literal punishment but a hyperbole of severity: any fate is better than being the instrument of a little one’s spiritual destruction. Tōn mikrōn (the little ones) refers to disciples, the vulnerable members of the community.
3: prosechete heautois. ean hamartē ho adelphos sou, epitimēson autō, kai ean metanoesei, aphes autō — watch yourselves. If a brother or sister sins against you, rebuke them; and if they repent, forgive them. The self-watching command (prosechete heautois) precedes the community-ethics instruction: the community member must first examine their own standing before administering discipline or forgiveness. Epitimēson (rebuke, reprove — the same word used for Jesus’s exorcistic rebukes) — the rebuke is not punitive but corrective; it aims at metanoia (repentance, change of direction). Repentance is the condition for forgiveness; the rebuke creates the opportunity for both.
4: kai ean heptakis tēs hēmeras hamart��sē eis se kai heptakis epistrepsē pros se legōn: metanow, aphēseis autō — even if they sin against you seven times in a day and seven times come back saying, I repent, you must forgive them. Seven times (heptakis) — the number of completeness; the forgiveness demand is unlimited in principle, not merely generous. Matthew’s version (77 times or 70 x 7, Matthew 18:21-22) makes the same point with larger numbers. Aphēseis (you will forgive — future indicative, the strongest imperative form in Greek) — the forgiveness is required, not optional. The community that can forgive without limit is the embodiment of the limitless forgiveness of God.
5: kai eipan hoi apostoloi tō kyriō: prosthes hēmin pistin — the apostles said to the Lord: Increase our faith! The apostles’ request is understandable: the demand for unlimited forgiveness has exceeded their sense of personal resource. Prosthes hēmin pistin (add to us faith) — the request treats faith as a quantifiable resource that can be augmented. Jesus’s response will challenge this framing: it is not the quantity of faith that matters but its object; even the smallest genuine faith has power beyond their expectations.
6: ei echete pistin hōs kokkon sinapeōs, elegete an tē sykaminō tautē: ekrizōthēti kai phyteuth��ti en tē thalassē — if you have faith as small as a mustard seed, you can say to this mulberry tree: be uprooted and planted in the sea. The mustard-seed comparison (also Matthew 17:20 with a mountain; Mark 11:23 with a mountain) here is applied to a sykaminos (mulberry tree, not fig or sycamore — a tree with deep roots and long lifespan). Kai hypēkousen an hymin (and it would obey you) — the divine passive behind the obedience: nature obeys the faith-word because nature obeys God, and faith aligned with God participates in that obedience. The disciples’ problem is not the quantity of their faith but its direction.
7: tis de ex hymōn doulon echōn arotriōnta ē poimainonta — suppose one of you has a servant plowing or looking after the sheep. The Unworthy Servant parable (unique to Luke) addresses the disciples’ attitude toward their own obedience after the unlimited-forgiveness command. The parable depicts a culturally normal master-slave relationship: the slave works in the field all day, then comes in to serve dinner before eating himself. No extraordinary demand is being made — this is simply the normal expectation of slavery.
8: all ouchi erei autō: hetoimason ti deipnēsō kai perizōsamenos diakonei moi heōs phagō kai piō — won’t the master say: prepare my supper, get ready and wait on me while I eat and drink; and after that you may eat and drink? The master’s expectation is sequential: serve first, eat after. The slave’s schedule is subordinated to the master’s needs without the master feeling any obligation to reverse the order or express gratitude. The parable is describing a normal social reality, not an ideal one; Jesus will use it to make a point about the attitude appropriate to disciples, not to endorse slavery.
9: mē echei charin tō doulō hoti epoiēsen ta diatachthenta; ou dokō — does he thank the servant because he did what he was told to do? I think not. Charin (grace, gratitude, thanks — the same word as “grace” in theological usage) — the master owes no extraordinary gratitude for a slave doing what slaves are supposed to do. The point is not the master’s virtue but the slave’s role: gratitude is appropriate when someone goes beyond obligation, not when they simply meet it.
10: houtōs kai hymeis, hotan poiēsēte panta ta diatachthenta hymin, legete hoti douloi achreioi esmen — so you also, when you have done everything you were told to do, should say: we are unworthy servants; we have only done our duty. Achreioi (unworthy, useless, unprofitable — the word for something that has produced no surplus beyond what was required) — the disciples who have obeyed fully have only met the baseline requirement; they have created no surplus of merit. The parable dismantles any foundation for merit-theology: covenant obedience is the minimum expected of a covenant member, not the basis for reward claims beyond grace.
11: kai egeneto en tō poreuesthai eis Ierousalēm kai autos diērcheto dia meson Samareias kai Galilaias — now on his way to Jerusalem, Jesus traveled along the border between Samaria and Galilee. The geographical notation (between Samaria and Galilee) sets the scene for the Samaritan leper’s appearance; the border region is where social mixing occurred between Jews and Samaritans. Poreuesthai eis Ierousalēm (traveling to Jerusalem) — Luke’s journey-narrative motif (9:51 through Luke 19:44) frames all the travel-narrative teaching within the deliberate movement toward the passion.
12: kai eiserchomenou autou eis tina kōmēn ēntēsan autō deka leproi andres — as he was going into a village, ten men who had leprosy met him. Leproi (those with leprosy — the OT term tsara’at covered a range of skin conditions rendering one ritually unclean and socially excluded, Lev 13-14). Hoi estēsan porrōthen (they stood at a distance) — the legal requirement of distance (m. Negaim Luke 13:12) meant lepers could not approach uninfected people; they called out from a prescribed separation. The ten are a mixed group as Luke 17:16 will reveal: nine Jews and one Samaritan.
13: kai autoi ēran phōnēn legontes: Iēsou epistata, eleēson hēmas — they called out: Jesus, Master, have pity on us! Epistata (master, one who stands over, superintendent — a Lukan title for Jesus, appearing 6 times in Luke and nowhere else in the NT) replaces the didaskalos (teacher) or kyrios (Lord) of other traditions. The plural eleēson hēmas (have pity on us) is a single petition from a group whose shared condition has overridden their social-ethnic distinctions: Jew and Samaritan together cry for mercy.
14: kai idōn eipen autois: poreutheientes epideixate heautous tois hiereusin — when he saw them, he said: go, show yourselves to the priests. The instruction follows Leviticus 14:1-32 (the ritual for the cleansed leper to present to the priest who certifies the cure and performs the purification rite). Kai egeneto en tō hypagein autous ekathristhēsan (and it happened that as they went they were cleansed) — the healing is not instantaneous at the command but progressive during obedient departure: faith expressed as action is met by healing during the action. The obedience precedes the sign.
15: heis de ex autōn idōn hoti iathē hypostrepsen meta phōnēs megalēs doxazōn ton theon — one of them, when he saw he was healed, turned back, praising God in a loud voice. The single turning-back (hypostrepsen) is emphasized: nine continue their journey; one reverses course. Meta phōnēs megalēs doxazōn ton theon (with a loud voice glorifying God) — the loud praise is public, the attribution (ton theon) is correct: the glory goes to God, not to the human healer. The gratitude-response is precisely what the other nine’s absence marks as absent.
16: kai epesen epi prosōpon para tous podas autou eucharistōn autō, kai autos ēn Samarit��s — he threw himself at Jesus’ feet and thanked him — and he was a Samaritan. The prostration (epesen epi prosōpon, fell on his face) is the posture of worship; the giving of thanks (eucharistōn — the source of the word “Eucharist”) is directed to Jesus personally. Luke’s climactic identification “and he was a Samaritan” (autos ēn Samaritēs) is the narrative shock: the grateful worshiper is the one the audience would have expected to be least responsive to a Jewish healer.
17: apokritheis de ho Iēsous eipen: ouchi hoi deka ekathristhēsan; hoi de ennea pou — Jesus asked: Were not all ten cleansed? Where are the other nine? The rhetorical question is not a complaint but a diagnostic: the nine have received healing and gone their way; the one has received healing and returned with worship. The question “where are the other nine?” hangs unanswered, pointed at both the nine and at the reader: what does receiving divine mercy without gratitude-return look like?
18: ouch heurethēsan hypostrepsantes dounai doxan tō theō ei mē ho allogenēs houtos — has no one returned to give praise to God except this foreigner? Allogenēs (of another race, foreigner — a term from inscriptions on the Jerusalem temple warning Gentiles not to enter the inner courts) is the strongest term for outsider status. Jesus uses the temple-exclusion word for the one person who has returned to give glory to God — the irony is complete: the temple-outsider performs the temple-worship (giving glory to God) that the nine with temple-access have failed to perform.
19: kai eipen autō: anastas poreuou; hē pistis sou sesōken se — then he said to him: Rise and go; your faith has saved you. Sesōken se (has saved you) — the same formula as Luke 7:50 (sinful woman), Luke 8:48 (hemorrhaging woman), and Luke 18:42 (blind Bartimaeus). The other nine were cleansed (ekathristhēsan, ritual purity restored); this one is saved (sesōken, the comprehensive term covering physical, spiritual, and social restoration). The distinction is Luke’s Christological point: healing is available to all ten; saving is available to those who return in faith and worship.
20: eperoētheis de hypo tōn Pharisaiōn pote erchetai hē basileia tou theou, apekrithē autois kai eipen — once, on being asked by the Pharisees when the kingdom of God would come, Jesus replied. The Pharisees’ question (pote erchetai hē basileia tou theou, when is the kingdom of God coming?) operates within the framework of Jewish apocalyptic expectation: the kingdom as a future observable political event. Meta paratērēseōs (with observation, with visible signs to be watched for) — paratērēsis is a medical/astronomical term for the careful observation of symptoms or heavenly bodies; the Pharisees want a timetable that can be calculated from visible indicators.
22: eipen de pros tous mathētas: eleusontai hēmerai hote epithymēsete mian tōn hēmerōn tou huiou tou anthrōpou idein kai ouk opsesthe — he said to his disciples: The time is coming when you will long to see one of the days of the Son of Man, but you will not see it. The warning to disciples (private, unlike the public response to the Pharisees) addresses the temptation toward false messianic speculation during persecution. Epithymēsete (you will long for, desire intensely) — in times of suffering, the disciples will crave the immediate return and vindication of the Son of Man; Jesus warns that this desire will leave them vulnerable to false claimants.
23: kai erousin hymin: idou ekei; ē idou hōde; mē apelthēte mēde diōxēte — people will tell you, There he is! or Here he is! Do not go running off after them. The warning against following false localized claims about the Son of Man’s presence echoes the sign-refusal of the Pharisees (Luke 17:20): the genuine coming of the Son of Man will not be observed in a specific location — it will be universal and unmistakable. Mē apelthēte mēde diōxēte (do not go, do not pursue) — the imperative negative commands: the disciples are to resist the geographic magnetism of false messianic claims.
24: hōsper gar hē astrapē astraptousa ek tēs hypo ton ouranon eis tēn hyp ouranon lampei, houtōs estai ho huios tou anthrōpou en tē hēmera autou — for the Son of Man in his day will be like the lightning, which flashes and lights up the sky from one end to the other. Astrapē (lightning) — instantaneous, simultaneous across the entire sky, impossible to miss or localize. The image answers the false claimants of Luke 17:23: the real coming of the Son of Man will require no announcement, no specific location, and no pursuit. It will be self-evidently universal. The comparison establishes the eschatological character of the Parousia: it will have the quality of a lightning flash — total, immediate, cosmic.
25: prōton de dei auton polla pathein kai apodokimasthēnai apo tēs geneas tautēs — but first he must suffer many things and be rejected by this generation. The insertion of the passion prediction into the eschatological discourse is Luke’s characteristic linkage of suffering and glory: before the lightning-flash of the Parousia comes the darkness of the passion. Dei (it is necessary — divine necessity) frames the suffering as the required path, not an accident or setback. Apodokimasthēnai (to be rejected — the word for a stone failing the builder’s inspection, Psalms 118:22 LXX) — the rejection is as specific as the suffering is general.
26: kai kathōs egeneto en tais hēmerais Nōe, houtōs estai kai en tais hēmerais tou huiou tou anthrōpou — just as it was in the days of Noah, so also will it be in the days of the Son of Man. The Noah-comparison and the Lot-comparison (Luke 17:26-30) are not about wickedness primarily but about normality: the people of Noah’s and Lot’s day were going about ordinary life — eating, drinking, marrying, buying, selling, planting, building. The judgment did not arrive during a moral crisis obvious to observers; it arrived during business as usual.
27: ēsthion, epinon, egamoun, egamizonto, achri hēs hēmeras eisēlthen Nōe eis tēn kibōton — people were eating, drinking, marrying and being given in marriage up to the day Noah entered the ark. The six activities (eating, drinking, marrying, being given in marriage, building, selling) are all neutral — lawful human activities. The judgment that interrupted them was not a response to a specific escalation of sin but the arrival of the predetermined moment. The lesson is not that normal life is sinful but that normal life’s continuation should not be taken as evidence that judgment is not imminent.
28: homoiōs kathōs egeneto en tais hēmerais Lōt: ēsthion, epinon, ēgorazon, epōloun, ephyteuon, ��kodomoun — it was the same in the days of Lot. People were eating and drinking, buying and selling, planting and building. The Lot-comparison adds four more activities (buying, selling, planting, building) to the Noah-list, extending the pattern: ordinary economic and agricultural life was uninterrupted until the moment of destruction. Sodom’s sin is not specifically named here (the context in Genesis is sexual violation of the divine messengers, but also pride and inhospitality, Ezekiel 16:49); the focus is on the abrupt interruption of normal life.
29: hē de hēmera exēlthen Lōt apo Sodōmōn, ebreksen pyr kai theion ap ouranou kai apōlesen pantas — but the day Lot left Sodom, fire and sulfur rained down from heaven and destroyed them all. Pyr kai theion (fire and sulfur — the Genesis 19:24 judgment language) — the sudden totality of the destruction mirrors the lightning-flash suddenness of the Son of Man’s day. Apōlesen pantas (destroyed them all) — the universality of the destruction within the condemned city; no one remaining in Sodom survived. The escape of Lot (and his daughters) is the paradigm of rescue by departure before judgment falls.
30: kata ta auta estai hē hēmera ho huios tou anthrōpou apokalyptetai — it will be just like this on the day the Son of Man is revealed. Apokalyptetai (is revealed, is uncovered — the same root as apokalypsis, revelation) — the Parousia is a revelation/uncovering, not merely a return: what was hidden (the Son of Man’s glory) will be made manifest. The Noah-Lot comparisons frame it as the arrival of a predetermined moment during which ordinary life will be interrupted. The warning is to live with readiness rather than absorption in the ordinary.
31: en ekeinē tē hēmera hos estai epi tou dōmatos kai ta skeuē autou en tē oikia, mē katabatō arai auta — on that day no one on the housetop with possessions inside should go down to get them. The urgency of departure without retrieval echoes the Exodus instructions (no time to let bread rise, Exodus 12:11) and the Lot-escape (don’t look back, Genesis 19:17). The housetop-to-inside movement would cost precious seconds in a moment requiring instant flight. The possessions are dispensable; the moment of the Son of Man’s day is not.
32: mnēmoneuete tēs gynaikos Lōt — remember Lot’s wife! The single-sentence command is the most economical warning in the Synoptics. Lot’s wife looked back (Genesis 19:26) and became a pillar of salt — a monument to the deadly attraction of the condemned life she was leaving. The look was more than curiosity: it was the expression of attachment to what was being left behind. In the eschatological context, looking back is the failure to commit fully to the departure that the Son of Man’s day requires.
33: hos ean zētēsē tēn psychēn autou peripoiēsasthai apolesei autēn, hos d an apolesē zōsai autēn — whoever tries to keep their life will lose it, and whoever loses their life will preserve it. The life-losing-life paradox (also Luke 9:24; Mark 8:35; Matthew 16:25; John 12:25) is placed in the eschatological context: on the day of the Son of Man, those who prioritize self-preservation will lose everything; those who have released their grip on life will find it preserved. Psychēn here is both physical life and the whole person/self; the paradox operates at both levels.
34: legō hymin, tautē tē nykti esontai dyo epi klinēs mias, ho heis paralēmphthēsetai kai ho heteros aphethēsetai — I tell you, on that night two people will be in one bed; one will be taken and the other left. The three examples of division (bed-partners, grain-grinders, field-workers) illustrate the selective nature of the eschatological separation: proximity — sharing a bed, a millstone, a field — does not create identical destiny. The divine selection is not visible in advance from external circumstances. Paralēmphthēsetai (will be taken) and aphethēsetai (will be left/released) — which is better is not specified in Luke; the context of the Son of Man’s coming may imply the taken are gathered to him.
35: esontai dyo alēthouaai epi to auto, hē mia paralēmphthēsetai hē de hetera aphethēsetai — two women will be grinding grain together; one will be taken and the other left. The grinding-together image is of two women working the same millstone in close cooperation; their shared labor does not predict shared destiny in the eschatological separation. The pattern reinforces Luke 17:34’s point: physical proximity, shared work, and common circumstances are not determinative of eschatological outcome. The hidden differentiation of readiness/faith is what matters.
36: duo en tō agrō, ho heis paralēmphthēsetai kai ho heteros aphethēsetai — two men will be in the field; one will be taken and the other left. This verse is absent from several important manuscripts (including P75, B, and the Sahidic Coptic) and was likely added from the parallel in Matthew 24:40; Luke’s authentic text may end the series with Luke 17:35. If original, it completes a triad (bed/house, mill/house, field) covering rest, domestic work, and agricultural labor — all spheres of ordinary life in which the eschatological division will occur.
37: kai apokrithentes legousin autō: pou, kyrie; ho de eipen autois: hopou to sōma, ekei kai hoi aetoi episynachthēsontai — where, Lord? he replied: Where there is a dead body, there the eagles will gather. The disciples’ locational question (“where?”) receives a proverbial answer that resists pinning down a geographic location. Aetoi (eagles or vultures — the Greek aetos can mean either; vultures are the more likely image since they gather around corpses) — the carrion-birds find the corpse without needing directions; the Son of Man’s coming will be self-evidently obvious in the same way. The parable-proverb also carries a darker connotation: the judgment of the Son of Man’s day will find its object as surely as vultures find a carcass.
